summary: Patrick McKenzie announces his book on conversion optimization, SEO, and A/B testing for software companies, published through Hyperink. The book compiles approximately 20 of his best blog essays with 4 new essays, covering selling software, increasing conversions, and SEO strategy. He explains he avoided traditional publishing because the economics didn't make sense, but Hyperink's more equitable royalty model and modern workflow convinced him. The book targets solo software entrepreneurs, SaaS marketing/engineering teams, and anyone who wanted his blog distilled into a curated collection.
key_points: Traditional publishing economics are broken for niche technical authors — a likely $5,000 advance that fails to earn out isn't worth months of work
Hyperink represents a Publishing 2.0 model with more equitable royalty splits and workflows built on modern tools rather than legacy publishing infrastructure
The book includes never-before-seen accounts of abysmal failure, because failure teaches as much as success
The target audience is practitioners — people with working products who need to optimize marketing outcomes, not people still building products
money_quotes: The notion of spending months of work with a publisher to write a book that would likely fail to earn-out a $5,000 advance seemed to be a silly thing to do just to put "published author" on my resume.
Royalties are split more equitably than 93-7-but-with-accounting-practices-that-would-make-the-RIAA-proud.
I have the design sense of an addlebrained squirrel who fell into the Christmas eggnog and drowned.
Rather than reading through 600 posts in chronological order, could you just distill your blog down into the best twenty posts and categorize them for me? My time isn't totally valueless.
Never-before-seen tales of abysmal failure, because that sometimes teaches as much as the successes.

If you've considered trying your hand at writing a book but, like me, thought the traditional publishing industry is largely toxic and exploitative by construction, I'd encourage you to give them a whirl.
key_insight: Traditional publishing economics are misaligned with niche technical expertise, but curating existing high-value content through modern publishing platforms can create a viable book without the typical author exploitation.
